OCR Language Paper 1 Section B — 10 Most Likely Questions + Skeleton Plans (2026)

For: OCR GCSE English Language, Paper 1 Section B (J351/01), "Communicating Information and Ideas". Section B is Question 5 or Question 6 — you choose ONE and write a single extended piece. 40 marks — AO5 (Communication and Organisation) 24 marks; AO6 (Technical Accuracy) 16 marks. Target on the page: 650+ words.

A note on the framework. Sophicly's IUMVCC persuasive structure and its element-by-element scoring apply universally across every exam board. OCR's Section B rewards the same two things every other board rewards: AO5 communication and organisation, and AO6 technical accuracy. What you are reading is Sophicly's universal IUMVCC framework applied to OCR's J351/01 mark scheme. The structure does not change from board to board; only the printed form, audience and statement do.

Source. The themes below are Sophicly's verified 2026 OCR forecast, distilled from The Complete Writer's Craft — Creative Non-Fiction (Verified Past-Paper Writing Prompts section, OCR J351/01 table of statements, forms and audiences). The forecast applies a recurrence-cycle analysis to OCR's own Section B cluster pattern plus current-affairs salience for a 2026 candidate.

What makes OCR Paper 1 distinctive. J351/01 reading section gives you two non-fiction texts on a shared theme — one nineteenth-century, one contemporary — and the Section B writing topic loosely echoes that theme. OCR's Section B is also the only board that uses blog posts and online forum posts alongside the usual talk, speech, article and letter. OCR has never set a report or a formal essay in Section B, so neither is modelled here. Letters at OCR are addressed to a wide range: an employer, a friend, a newspaper editor, or a well-known person you admire.

Statistics and sources. Every numeric claim and named source cited below has been verified against UK government, regulator, charity or peer-reviewed sources (Ofcom, NHS, Met Office, Sport England, WRAP, WWF, UNEP, Mental Health Foundation, IFS and similar). OCR examiners penalise invented statistics; everything here is real and citeable in exam conditions. Where a theme needs a figure that could not be verified, the claim is written and then marked [VERIFY-NEEDED] — never state an unverified number as fact.

Variety of openings — no two model answers start the same way. A top-band answer is not just a correctly structured IUMVCC; it opens in a way that is creative and fits the topic. There is no single "correct" opening. The model pieces below deliberately use a different hook archetype each time, so you see the range — and so the document never teaches one formula. The hooks are: a question, an "imagine" scenario, a metaphor, an anecdote, a shocking statistic, a bold statement, a contrast, an assumption-then-reversal, and repetition. Pick whichever genuinely suits the statement OCR prints — not whichever you rehearsed. Forcing a clever opening onto the wrong topic loses more than it gains.

No author impersonation. Never write "in the voice of" a named real person. Vary openings via the hook archetypes only.

Opening-hook map — what each model answer demonstrates (so you see the full range):

Q Topic Form Hook archetype used What it demonstrates
Q1 Appearance / identity Article Historical contrast (then vs now) Two eras side by side to expose what has changed
Q2 Environment Speech Extended metaphor (the topic as a machine) A long, controlled sentence building one image
Q3 Technology Article Assumption set up, then reversed "You have heard… in fact the opposite is true"
Q4 Person you admire Letter Personal anecdote A concrete memory that earns the whole piece
Q5 Health / food Talk Concrete contrast ("Picture two plates…") Inequality made visible by two images
Q6 Personal achievement Blog post Sensory snapshot A single image written like a paragraph of a novel
Q7 Success / advice Speech Bold statement A claim with no qualifier, then earned
Q8 Travel complaint Letter Shocking statistic A number that overturns assumption
Q9 Animals / nature Online forum post Rhetorical question A question only the reader can answer
Q10 A moment that changed me Article Imagine scenario "Picture this" plus a single decisive moment

These are illustrations, not prescriptions. The same topic could open a different way and score just as highly — provided the choice fits. Study the variety; copy the range, not any single example. Each piece still runs IUMVCC underneath. The architecture does not change; the opening does. That variety is what inventive structure (AO5 top band) rewards.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

  1. A predicted theme for OCR P1 Section B in 2026, with three plausible stem variants (different form / audience / statement combinations).
  2. A frame — the controlling argument that holds your whole answer together.
  3. Three hook options so you can pick the archetype that suits the stem OCR actually prints.
  4. A skeleton IUMVCC plan with sentence-level guidance for every beat.
  5. Three counter-argument variants so the strongest objection the printed prompt invites is the one you concede.
  6. A voice and banned-patterns check to run before you submit.

The plan structure stays identical across all ten questions. You rehearse the shell once; on the day, you swap in the surface prompt's audience, form and statement of opinion. This is the same discipline that powers your Literature answers — memorise the load-bearing structure, recombine for the printed question.

Full IUMVCC walkthrough: Sophicly Persuasive Writing Guide.

Memorise this once — the IUMVCC plan shape

Sophicly's transactional persuasive structure has SIX beats. Memorise the beat name, the word target, and the dominant move for each one.

Beat Words Sentences Dominant move
I — Introduction 50–100 2–3 Hook + topic bridge. No facts list. Build an image.
U — Urgency 100–150 3–4 Metaphor-first. Why this matters NOW.
M — Methodology 250–350 9–12 (three sub-points) Each sub-point: point → metaphor → development → evidence. Organic transitions only.
V — Vision 100–150 3 Three escalating sentences painting the better future.
C — Counter-argument 75–100 2–3 Concede the strongest objection. Refute with one decisive pivot.
C — Conclusion 75–100 2–3 Circular echo of the Introduction image, or a climactic final picture.

Total on the page: ~650–850 words. AO5 reward = inventive structure that does not feel formulaic. AO6 reward = controlled vocabulary, sentence variety, accurate SPaG.

The eight Sophicly hooks (pick ONE per piece)

OCR reward variety; pick the archetype that suits the audience the stem specifies.

  1. Counterintuitive fact — a number that overturns assumption.
  2. Personal anecdote — a two-sentence concrete memory.
  3. Sensory snapshot — a single image written like a paragraph from a novel.
  4. Imagine scenario — Picture this: + a future or hypothetical moment.
  5. Rhetorical question — a question only the reader can answer.
  6. Bold statement — a claim, no qualifier.
  7. Contrast / paradox — two clauses that crash against each other.
  8. Extended metaphor — a single comparison the rest of the piece returns to.

MADFATHER'S CROPS — the floor, not the ceiling

You need two or three devices working together at every persuasive moment. Single-device sentences score low on AO5. MADFATHER'S CROPS is an aide-memoire — it lists the techniques you should never forget, not every technique you are allowed to use.

The MADFATHER'S CROPS aide-memoire:

  • M Metaphor / A Alliteration / D Direct address / F Facts + foreshadowing / A Assonance / T Triadic structure / H Hyperbole / E Emotive language / R Rhetorical questions / C Contrast / R Repetition / O Onomatopoeia / P Personification / S Simile + sibilance.

Beyond MADFATHER'S CROPS — the wider technique bank. Top-band AO5 answers reach for techniques the aide-memoire does not list. Learn the names and the shapes; use them where the topic invites them.

  • Anaphora — repeated word at the start of consecutive clauses ("We have the science. We have the funding. We have the time we are not using."). Repetition that builds pressure.
  • Epistrophe — repeated word at the END of consecutive clauses ("Reduce the bag. Repair the shelf. Refuse the bag.").
  • Polysyndeton — repeated "and" connecting items ("The kit and the strip and the seat and the ticket."). Slows the reader down; emphasises accumulation.
  • Asyndeton — list with the conjunctions deliberately removed ("Reduce, repair, refuse."). Speeds the reader up; emphasises compression.
  • Polysyllabic listing — a list of multi-syllable words for cumulative weight ("relentless, industrialised, unregulated, profitable.").
  • Parallelism — same grammatical shape repeated ("Move the bell. Move the calendar. Move the system."). Anaphora + parallelism is a top-band combination.
  • Antithesis — two ideas balanced in matched grammar ("The screen offers the world; the screen withholds the room.").
  • Chiasmus — A-B-B-A reversal ("Plates built for profit are now profit built on plates.").
  • Zeugma — one verb governing two objects, one literal and one figurative ("She lost her phone, her sleep, and her concentration in a single term.").
  • Climax / cumulative listing — list ordered by escalating intensity ("The walk, the hill, the summit, the year I learned I could finish.").
  • Bathos — sudden drop from the elevated to the trivial ("We are losing the glaciers, the coral reefs — and, less seriously, the Cornish coast in August.").
  • Periodic sentence — the main point withheld until the end ("Despite the filters, the algorithms, the engineered comparison and the absent regulation — the face survives.").
  • Cumulative sentence — main point first, then escalating detail ("The plate is the problem — the plate, the marketing behind it, the profit behind the marketing.").
  • Anastrophe — inverted word order ("Long the country has waited.").
  • Aporia — feigned uncertainty as a rhetorical move ("What does one even say to a generation raised on filters?").
  • Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person, idea or object ("Reader, you have done your share.").
  • Litotes — emphasis via understated negative ("This is not a small problem.").
  • Anadiplosis — last word of one clause becomes first word of the next ("We trust the label. The label trusts the supplier. The supplier trusts nobody.").

The point. A top-band answer does not just use techniques — it picks the technique that fits the IDEA. Anaphora suits mounting pressure; asyndeton suits decisive action; bathos suits ironic deflation; chiasmus suits paradox; polysyndeton suits oppressive accumulation. Match the technique to the meaning.

Effects on the Reader — Sophicly's 4-fold framework

Every beat in IUMVCC has a target effect on the reader:

  • I — Focus. What should the reader notice first?
  • U — Feel. What emotion grips them now?
  • M — Think. What argument is reshaping their judgement?
  • V — Feel + Act. What better future moves them?
  • C (counter) — Think. You have heard the objection; here is why it fails.
  • C (conclusion) — Act. The call that lands.

Name the Effect you are pursuing at every beat. Plans that only chase Think (logic) lose AO5 marks for failing to move the reader.

Banned patterns — they cost AO5 and AO6 marks

  • Shows / showing / shown anywhere in the piece. Replace with illustrates, demonstrates, emphasises, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, signals, enacts.
  • Abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to). Replace with a concrete image.
  • More than ten to be verbs across the piece (is / are / was / were). Use active verbs.
  • More than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.
  • Discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly … Secondly … Finally). Bridge with verbal connectors instead.
  • Triadic structures without parallel grammar.
  • Vague intensifiers (really, very, just, basically).
  • American register (move as a noun, gotten, trash). British English only.

Persuasive craft — principles, not quotas

This is creative writing. There are no numeric rules — no fixed number of rhetorical questions, no set quota of anecdotes, no device confined to particular beats. Aristotle warned against persuasion by "scattered persuasive factors" (technique by count); judge by craft instead. The principles below are how to judge a choice, never boxes to tick.

  • Integrate the three appeals (Aristotle): ethos (why trust the writer?), pathos (what should the reader feel?), logos (what is the actual reason?) — woven together, not one technique repeated.
  • Fit the piece to its audience and occasion: a forum post replying to an imagined thread is not a blog post for regular readers is not a speech performed aloud. Register is judged on AO5.
  • Concrete beats abstract: one specific image or named person moves a reader more than a general claim ("one boy, twelve, walks two hours for water", not "many people suffer").
  • Clarity is honesty (Orwell): prefer the short word, the active verb, the fresh image; cut every word that does no work.
  • Rhythm at the climax: repetition and three-part structures land hardest where the idea peaks — used by instinct, never to a count.
  • Call to action sits in the Conclusion. Make it specific (write to the company, change one habit this week), not vague (make a change).

Q1 — Appearance, judgement, identity

Q1 — Appearance, judgement, identity

Why this is likely. OCR set an appearance/judgement prompt in June 2024 and the cluster pairs cleanly with J351/01's nineteenth-century non-fiction archive (Victorian writing on dress, physiognomy and reputation) and contemporary journalism on filters, identity and self-worth. The statement-of-opinion frame fits an article for young readers exactly.

Three plausible stems (OCR prints ONE of these shapes):

  1. "We are taught not to judge a book by its cover. Then we spend every day judging each other by ours." Write an article for a magazine for young people titled "The Face We Show".
  2. "Filters have replaced the mirror. A generation now compares itself to faces that were never real." Write an article for a teenage website arguing your point of view.
  3. "Identity is who you are. Appearance is who an algorithm wants you to be." Write an article for a school magazine responding to this statement.

Frame. The body is not the problem. The mirror an industry built — and that an algorithm now polishes daily — is the problem. The reform target is the image-maker, not the teenager looking at the image.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Effect: Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Historical contrast: "A teenager in 1890 checked her reflection in a piece of glass that wanted nothing from her. A teenager in 2026 checks hers in an app that profits from her dissatisfaction."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "The face most teenagers compare themselves to is a face that has never existed in the world."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Form-room mirror. Lean in. Open the camera. Lean in again — this time at the face the app prefers."
  • Topic bridge: the comparison is industrial, not personal.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Effect: Feel)

  • Image: the filter as a hall of mirrors built by strangers.
  • Metaphor: "We are raising a generation in a hall of mirrors built by people they will never meet."
  • Extension: the changing room before PE; the lunch queue; the photo retaken because the first did not lie enough.
  • Evidence: Mental Health Foundation's 2019 body-image report — 34% of UK adults felt anxious because of their body image, rising above half among teenagers; Dove Self-Esteem Project research.
  • Devices: triadic (changing room / lunch queue / retaken photo); short pivot ("This is not vanity.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Effect: Think)

  • M1 — The platform. Frame: regulate the filter, not the child. Metaphor: "Label the edited photo the way we label the doctored advert." Develop: mandatory edited-image disclosure. Evidence: Norway's Marketing Control Act amendment (2021, in force 2022) requiring disclosure of digitally edited bodies.
  • M2 — The classroom. Frame: teach the trick. Metaphor: "Show the seam in the costume and the costume stops convincing." Develop: PSHE media-literacy units; live editing demonstrations. Evidence: Dove Self-Esteem Project curriculum; PSHE Association media-literacy guidance.
  • M3 — The voice. Frame: adults cannot out-shout the algorithm; they can teach the child to recognise it. Metaphor: "Give the child the spectacles, not just the mirror." Develop: tutor-time conversations; naming the trick the phone plays. Evidence: PSHE Association guidance on body image.
  • Transitions: The platform is one fix. The classroom is the second. The voice is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Effect: Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The mirror is honest again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The fourteen-year-old leaves the form room thinking about lunch, not her cheekbones."
  • Sentence 3 (5 words): "Childhood reclaims its own face."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Effect: Think) — three options

  • Always-was objection: "Teenagers have always worried about looks; the filter is just the new mirror."
  • Free-speech objection: "Regulating filters and adverts is censorship of an industry."
  • Self-esteem objection: "Confidence-building, not regulation, is the answer."
  • Refutation pivot: "There is a mirror, and there is a billion-pound industry standing behind that mirror with a sales pitch. The worry is not new. The seller is."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Effect: Act)

  • Circular echo: the form-room mirror; lean in, lean out.
  • New move: she leans out, and she leaves.
  • Final sentence: "Give the mirror back to the child. The child is enough."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine for young people, "The Face We Show", ~715 words) — opens with a historical-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Think about this. A fourteen-year-old in 1890 looked at herself in a dressing-table mirror her mother had bought. The mirror told her, more or less, the truth: a little kinder at the angle the gas lamp lit, a little harsher where her brother had knocked it. Either way, the mirror was a piece of glass. It had no interest in her dissatisfaction, because it had nothing to sell her.

A fourteen-year-old in 2026 looks at herself in a mirror engineered by a multi-billion-pound algorithm whose entire business depends on her dissatisfaction. We are told not to judge a book by its cover. We have built a cover that judges back.

[U — Urgency]

We are raising a generation in a hall of mirrors built by people they will never meet. The Mental Health Foundation's 2019 body-image report, the most authoritative UK research on this, found thirty-four percent of British adults had felt anxious because of their body image, with the figure climbing above half among teenagers. The changing room before PE, the lunch queue, the bathroom photo retaken because the first did not lie enough: each of these moments has industrialised. This is not vanity. This is a comparison engineered to fail, sold to a child who never asked to enter it.

[M1 — The platform]

Label the edited photo the way we label the doctored advert. Norway amended its Marketing Control Act in 2021 to require influencers and advertisers to disclose when an image of a body has been digitally altered; the law came into force in 2022, and the rest of Europe is still deciding whether to follow. The teenager scrolling tonight cannot tell which faces are real and which are built. The platforms profit from her not knowing. Regulate the image-maker. The child does not need re-educating; the seller does.

[M2 — The classroom]

Show the seam in the costume and the costume stops convincing. The Dove Self-Esteem Project, which delivers body-image lessons in over a hundred and fifty countries, demonstrates that teaching teenagers how a filtered image is actually made — what is smoothed, removed, added, made impossible — measurably reduces the harm those images cause. A single PSHE lesson, taught with a phone and a screen, can take a year off the damage. The classroom can do what the platform will not.

[M3 — The voice]

Adults cannot out-shout the algorithm; they can teach the child to recognise it. Give her the spectacles, not just the mirror. PSHE Association guidance on media literacy hands any tutor a starting script: how to ask, how to listen, how to name the trick the phone is playing. The algorithm has the advantage of repetition — it returns a thousand times a day, patient and tireless. A parent cannot match that volume, and should not try; what a parent can do is hand the child a single durable question to ask of every flawless face she scrolls past: who profits if I believe this is real? A fortnightly conversation, repeated for a year, will not silence the algorithm, but it leaves the teenager with a vocabulary the algorithm cannot take from her. The child who can name the lie is the child the lie cannot fully hold.

[V — Vision]

The mirror is honest again. The fourteen-year-old leaves the form room thinking about lunch, not her cheekbones. Childhood reclaims its own face.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say teenagers have always worried about their looks, and the filter is simply the new mirror. There is a mirror, and there is a billion-pound industry standing behind that mirror with a sales pitch. The worry is not new. The seller is. To pretend the difference between a Victorian dressing-table glass and a profit-engineered comparison engine is a difference of degree, not of kind, is to hand the industry exactly the silence it relies on.

[C — Conclusion]

So judge the cover, if you must. Then notice who designed it, who profits from your verdict, and who is still leaning into the form-room mirror tomorrow morning, comparing a real face to one that was never real. The mirrors are not the same. Stop pretending they are. Give the mirror back to the child. The child is enough.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article for a teenage website on filters replacing the mirror) and Stem 3 (article for a school magazine on identity versus algorithm). The historical-contrast hook fits all three; pick two unequal images and let them argue against each other. For a less comparative open, switch to the sensory-snapshot hook.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Hall-of-mirrors metaphor anchored I → U → V → Conclusion. No shows. No patriarchy / patriarchal (Sophicly uses industry / commercial design / social pressure substitutes). No Americanisms.

Q2 — Environment, sustainability, climate action

Q2 — Environment, sustainability, climate action

Why this is likely. J351/01's shared-theme reading section frequently pairs Victorian nature and exploration writing with contemporary climate journalism, priming a Section B environment prompt. OCR favours the talk and speech forms for community-spirited, civic topics, and a 2026 candidate sits inside a decade of record UK temperatures.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have run out of someone-else-must-act. Every household, every postcode, every weekday morning is now where the climate is decided." Write a talk to persuade your class to take individual climate action.
  2. "Personal recycling and meat-free Mondays will not save the planet. The problem is corporate, not individual." Write a speech for a community meeting arguing for or against.
  3. "It is governments, not citizens, who failed the climate. Stop guilting the household." Write a talk for your year group responding to this statement.

Frame. Individual climate action without policy is performance, but policy without citizen pressure is decoration. The Sophicly position holds both at once: the household votes with its choices, but the choice that matters most is the vote at the ballot box.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Extended metaphor: "The climate is not a crisis; it is a machine — currents, feedbacks, slow-moving forces, all of it pressed by exhaust and the daily theft of the commute."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Forty degrees in July. The tarmac softens. The radio reads a record temperature and the country shrugs, then drives to the beach."
  • Bold statement: "There is no household innocent of the climate."
  • Topic bridge: the climate is not a crisis to feel; it is a posture to take.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: climate change as a slow flood already inside the home.
  • Metaphor: "The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway."
  • Extension: the carpet damp; the wallpaper bubbling; the family pretending nothing.
  • Evidence: Met Office 2022 — the UK passed 40°C for the first time on record; Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report — off track for 2030; Carbon Brief on consumption emissions.
  • Devices: triadic (carpet / wallpaper / family); short pivot ("This is the room.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The household. Frame: the smallest unit of climate policy is the kitchen. Metaphor: "Your fridge is a small parliament." Develop: the meat-free week, the thermostat at nineteen, the boycotted brand. Evidence: WRAP 2024 packaging data.
  • M2 — The street. Frame: street-level pressure changes councils. Metaphor: "Pavements are policy waiting to happen." Develop: tree-planting petitions, school-streets schemes, insulation co-operatives. Evidence: Transport for London's School Streets programme now runs at around 1,000 schools and has cut morning traffic by up to half in monitored boroughs.
  • M3 — The ballot box. Frame: the vote is the loudest action a household can take. Metaphor: "A ballot is the household's longest lever." Develop: voting on council climate plans; pressuring MPs on the net-zero pathway. Evidence: Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report.
  • Transitions: The kitchen is one step. The street is the next. The ballot is the last.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The street has trees again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Children walk to a school that learned to switch its lights off."
  • Sentence 3 (2 words): "Britain breathes."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Corporate-only objection: "Individual action is a distraction; only corporate regulation matters."
  • Wealth objection: "Richer households can afford solar panels and electric cars; the poorest cannot, so the policy is unfair."
  • Despair objection: "Britain is a small share of global emissions; nothing we do moves the needle."
  • Refutation pivot: "The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore. Withdraw the consumer and the corporation moves. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. Both move together, or neither moves."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the machine running hot returns; the curve begins to bend.
  • New move: the household has chosen; the street has chosen; the ballot has chosen.
  • Final sentence: "The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (talk to persuade your class, ~720 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

The climate is not a crisis; it is a machine — a vast, intricate apparatus of ocean currents and stratospheric winds and the warm Atlantic drift that keeps this country green in February, all of it pressed by industry, by exhaust, by the small daily theft of the morning commute. Every fraction of a degree we have added to the average temperature is, behind the headline, the result of a machine running hot. The Met Office confirmed in 2022 that the United Kingdom had passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history. Behind that headline sat the apparatus.

[U — Urgency]

This is not slow. The 2024 Climate Change Committee progress report told Parliament that the United Kingdom is no longer on track to meet its 2030 emissions targets — that the existing pace of decarbonisation, the existing schedule of renewables, the existing transport fleet will not, on their current path, hit the legally binding target the country wrote into its own statute. Read that twice. The country set its own target and is preparing to miss it. Carbon Brief's data on consumption emissions confirm what the policy data implies: we are exporting our pollution faster than we are removing it.

[M1 — The household]

And yet. The kitchen — that small, low-power, daily-running engine — is also a machine, and the household-scale machine is the unit at which the country is built. Three meals a day, multiplied across millions of homes, multiplied across the year, produce the supply chain the nation drives. A meat-free week shifts measurable carbon out of that chain. WRAP reported in 2024 that the United Kingdom places more than two million tonnes of plastic packaging on the market every year, and the household is where most of it begins. The kitchen, multiplied, is policy in disguise.

[M2 — The street]

Pavements are policy waiting to happen. Q3 — Technology, invention, young lives

Q3 — Technology, invention, young lives

Why this is likely. J351/01 regularly pairs Victorian writing on new inventions (the telegraph, the railway, the camera) with contemporary technology journalism — a natural prime for a Section B technology prompt. OCR has never directly tested the smartphone-and-young-lives cluster, and current-affairs salience is high (the Online Safety Act 2023, the Smartphone Free Childhood movement, generative AI in classrooms).

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Every invention promises to free us. The smartphone freed us into a cage we now carry in our pockets." Write an article for a broadsheet newspaper arguing your point of view.
  2. "Stop blaming the technology. The problem is adults who never set limits and schools that never modelled focus." Write an article for a magazine responding to this statement.
  3. "Artificial intelligence will not replace young people. It will replace the young people who never learned to think without it." Write an article for a website for young readers.

Frame. Technology is not the enemy. Design without conscience is. Every feature engineered for maximum attention is engineered against the developing teenage brain. The reform target is not the user; it is the design.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Assumption-then-reversal: "You have heard that the smartphone connects the lonely teenager to the world. In fact it has built the loneliness it then sells the cure for."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Ofcom found that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life outside school."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Half-past midnight. The bedroom is dark; the face is lit blue. The thumb keeps scrolling, because it has forgotten the brain it belongs to."
  • Topic bridge: design that profits from attention is design that costs childhood.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the attention economy as a casino built for children.
  • Metaphor: "Every notification is a slot-machine handle pulled in a child's pocket, and every win is engineered to lose."
  • Extension: small dopamine wins; escalating spend; the family table emptying of attention.
  • Evidence: Ofcom 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes — 12–17s spend 4+ hours a day online; Online Safety Act 2023.
  • Devices: triadic (pull / win / lose); short pivot ("This is not entertainment.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Design, not user. Frame: regulation must target the engineers, not the children. Metaphor: "We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards." Develop: infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks. Evidence: Online Safety Act 2023 duties on platforms.
  • M2 — School as sanctuary. Frame: classrooms protected from device intrusion outperform classrooms that surrender. Metaphor: "A school without phones is a school where attention can breathe." Develop: phone-free pilots; restored eye contact. Evidence: the Smartphone Free Childhood movement (2024).
  • M3 — AI literacy. Frame: teach the model as a tool, not a ghost. Metaphor: "Teach the calculator and the child still learns the maths." Develop: AI literacy as a curriculum strand; disclosure rules. Evidence: JISC 2024 — over half of UK students used generative AI for academic work; Finland's 2023 curriculum framework on AI literacy.
  • Transitions: The design is one half. The classroom is the second. The literacy is the third.

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The bedside table holds books."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Sixteen-year-olds argue out loud the paragraphs they will write."
  • Sentence 3 (2 words): "Childhood breathes."

  • Connection objection: "Smartphones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a broadsheet newspaper, ~720 words) — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

You have heard that the smartphone is the great connector. You have heard it every time an advert promises a teenager a thousand friends in her pocket, every time a platform describes itself as a community, every time an adult, defending the device, reminds you that a lonely fourteen-year-old can now find people who understand her at three in the morning. You have heard, in short, that the phone cures isolation.

Except that is the wrong way round. The phone does not cure the loneliness; it manufactures it, then sells the cure. The connection is real. The loneliness underneath it, engineered by the very design that offers the remedy, is realer.

[U — Urgency]

Every notification is a slot-machine handle pulled in a child's pocket, and every win is engineered to lose. Ofcom's 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes report, the most authoritative UK dataset on this, finds that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life outside school. The dopamine win, the escalating spend, the family table emptying of attention one downturned face at a time: this is not entertainment. This is an attention economy that learned, years ago, that a child's evening was a market.

[M1 — Design, not user]

The instinct, when something goes wrong with a child, is to regulate the child. The instinct is wrong here. We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards. The Online Safety Act of 2023 finally accepted the principle that the platform, not the pupil, carries the duty of care. Infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks, the gentle dopamine architecture of a hundred small wins ordered to mask one larger loss: each was built. None is an accident. Regulate the boardroom, not the bedroom.

[M2 — School as sanctuary]

A school without phones is a school where attention can breathe. The Smartphone Free Childhood movement, started by two British mothers in 2024, has signed parental pacts in tens of thousands of households, and the schools that follow report the same quiet pattern: lunch halls talking again, corridors remembering eye contact, lessons holding for forty minutes instead of forty seconds. The phones are not banned from existence. They are asked to wait outside the room where the learning lives.

[M3 — AI literacy]

Teach the model as a tool, not a ghost. Teach the calculator and the child still learns the maths. JISC's 2024 survey of UK students found that more than half had used generative AI for academic work, while detection remained inconsistent across institutions. The instinct of frightened schools is to forbid, and forbidding has a perfect record of failure: prohibition has never once removed a technology a generation found useful, only driven it underground where no adult could teach its limits. Finland's national curriculum framework, updated in 2023, treats AI literacy as a cross-curricular competency — not a feared intrusion, but a literacy developed alongside reading and writing. Pupils there learn what the model is, what it cannot do, and how to disclose when they have used it. Banning the machine produces secret use. Teaching it produces honest use. Choose the second.

[V — Vision]

The bedside table holds books. Sixteen-year-olds argue out loud the paragraphs they will write. Childhood breathes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say the workplace already uses these tools, so banning them in school is Luddism. The workplace uses calculators too; schools still teach long division. The point of school is not to mimic the workplace; it is to build the mind the workplace will, eventually, employ. A graduate who can prompt a machine without thinking is a graduate the machine does not need to employ.

[C — Conclusion]

So believe, if you must, that the phone is the great connector. Then look at the bedroom at half-past midnight: the dark room, the blue light, the thumb that has forgotten the brain it belongs to. The connection is on the screen. The loneliness was built around it. The thumb can stop. The phone can go downstairs. The dark can hold rest instead of the algorithm. Childhood is not a market. Stop selling it.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article arguing the crisis is adults and schools, not technology) and Stem 3 (article for young readers on AI replacing those who never learned to think). The assumption-then-reversal hook works in all three; set up the promise, then dismantle it. For Stem 3, lead with M3.

Q4 — A letter to a person you admire

Q4 — A letter to a person you admire

Why this is likely. OCR's Section B letter options uniquely include a letter "to a well-known person" or to someone you admire, and the cluster pairs with the paper's archive of nineteenth-century memoir and biographical writing. The form invites personal voice and a single sustained address — exactly what AO5 rewards when controlled.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write a letter to a person you admire, explaining the difference they have made to the way you see the world."
  2. "Some people are admired for what they own. The people worth admiring are admired for what they gave away." Write a letter to someone you admire, arguing why their example matters.
  3. "Write a letter to a person whose work or example has changed how you intend to live."

Frame. Admiration is not applause; it is inheritance. The people worth admiring hand us a way of living, not a list of achievements — and the letter's job is to name the exact thing inherited, not to flatter.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Personal anecdote: "I was eleven the first time I watched you refuse to give up on something everyone else had already abandoned. I did not understand, then, that I was watching a decision I would copy for the rest of my life."
  • Bold statement: "I admire you for the smallest thing you ever did, and you will not remember doing it."
  • Rhetorical question: "How do you thank someone for a habit they handed you without noticing?"
  • Topic bridge: name the exact quality inherited, not a general praise.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the admired person as a quiet architect of a life they never saw built.
  • Metaphor: "You laid a foundation I have been building on ever since, without ever visiting the site."
  • Extension: the moment; the lesson hidden inside it; the years it has paid out.
  • Evidence: personal-narrative theme — no statistic required. Anchor in vivid, specific memory instead.
  • Devices: triadic (the moment / the lesson / the years); short pivot ("That is what admiration is.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — What you did. Frame: the concrete action, told as a scene. Metaphor: "You made a small, ordinary choice that turned out to be the load-bearing wall." Develop: the specific event, rendered with sensory detail. Evidence: the single anecdote (this is its home — do not add a second).
  • M2 — What it taught. Frame: the principle the action carried. Metaphor: "The lesson travelled further than the moment." Develop: how the example reframed effort, kindness, or courage. Evidence: a second occasion, briefly, when you applied it yourself.
  • M3 — What it built. Frame: the life now lived differently. Metaphor: "You are in every decision I make that I am not ashamed of." Develop: the present-day choices that trace back to the example. Evidence: a concrete present habit.
  • Transitions: That is what you did. This is what it taught. This is what it built.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (6 words): "I will pass the lesson on."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Someone younger will inherit, from me, the habit you gave you."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "The example keeps travelling."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Modesty objection: "You would say you did nothing remarkable."
  • Distance objection: "We have never met; you do not know I exist."
  • Achievement objection: "Others admire you for far grander things than this."
  • Refutation pivot: "The grand things are why the world knows your name. The small thing is why I changed mine — the way I live it. Both are true. Only one reached me."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: return to the moment in the Introduction; the eleven-year-old watching.
  • New move: the eleven-year-old grew up and is now writing the letter, the habit fully inherited.
  • Final sentence: "Thank you for the foundation. I am still building on it. So, I think, will the next person."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (letter to a person you admire, ~715 words) — opens with a personal-anecdote hook

[I — Introduction]

Dear Mr Okafor,

I was eleven the first time I watched you stay behind. The school football team had lost nine to nil, the rain had not let up, and every other adult had already walked to the car park. You stood on the touchline with the boy who had let in most of the goals and talked to him, quietly, for twenty minutes, until he stopped crying and started laughing. I did not understand, then, that I was watching a decision I would copy for the rest of my life.

[U — Urgency]

You laid a foundation I have been building on ever since, without ever once visiting the site. I doubt you remember that afternoon. You have coached hundreds of children; that boy was one touchline among thousands. But the moment, the lesson hidden inside it, and the years it has quietly paid out to me are the reason I am writing now, eleven years later, to tell you what you built. That is what admiration is. It is not applause. It is inheritance.

[M1 — What you did]

You made a small, ordinary choice that turned out to be the load-bearing wall of how I treat people. You did not lecture the boy. You did not tell him to toughen up. You asked him a question about the second goal, then listened to the whole long answer, then asked another. By the time the rain eased, he was explaining a save he was proud of. You had turned a defeat into something he could carry. I stood ten feet away, soaked, watching a grown man take a child's worst afternoon seriously.

[M2 — What it taught]

The lesson travelled much further than the moment. I learned, that afternoon, that attention is the kindest thing one person can give another, and that it costs nothing but the patience to stay when leaving would be easier. I had assumed, before then, that kindness was something grand and rare, reserved for emergencies and visible to everyone. You taught me it was the opposite: small, quiet, unwitnessed, and offered most often to the person no one else has stayed for. Two years later I sat with a friend the night his parents separated, and I did not try to fix it. I asked questions. I listened to the long answers. I had no idea, until I started writing this, that I was copying a touchline in the rain.

[M3 — What it built]

You are in every decision I make that I am not ashamed of. When I volunteer now with the younger years at school, I do the thing you did: I stay behind, I ask the second question, I wait for the answer that matters. Last term a Year Seven boy missed an open goal and sat apart at the edge of the pitch, and I recognised the scene before I recognised that I was about to repeat it. I sat down beside him and asked about the tackle he had won earlier. Within minutes he was on his feet again. The habit is so much part of me that I had forgotten where it came from. It came from you, on a touchline, in the rain, in front of an eleven-year-old you never noticed.

[V — Vision]

I will pass the lesson on. Someone younger will inherit, from me, the habit you gave me without meaning to. They will hand it, in turn, to a child neither of us will ever meet. The example keeps travelling.

[C — Counter-argument]

You would say, I think, that you did nothing remarkable — that you only spoke to an upset child, which any decent coach would do. Perhaps. But not every coach stayed in the rain, and not every child standing nearby was changed by it. The modesty is part of why the lesson landed; you taught it without knowing you were teaching, which is the only way the most important lessons ever arrive.

[C — Conclusion]

I was eleven, and you stayed behind. I am twenty-two now, and I have never stopped staying behind, because you made it look like the obvious thing to do. Thank you for the foundation. I am still building on it. So, I think, will the next person who watches me do the thing I once watched you do.

Yours, with more gratitude than this page can hold,

[the writer]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (a letter arguing why the example of someone who gave rather than owned matters) by sharpening M2 around generosity, and Stem 3 (a letter on a person whose work changed how you intend to live) by re-anchoring M3 in future intention. The personal-anecdote hook fits all three; this theme is narrative, so no statistic is needed — let the remembered scene do the persuading.

Q5 — Health, food, wellbeing

Q5 — Health, food, wellbeing

Why this is likely. J351/01's shared-theme reading pairs Victorian writing on diet, public health and the body with contemporary journalism on food, sleep and activity — a clean prime for a Section B wellbeing prompt. OCR favours the talk form for civic, persuade-your-peers topics.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have never known more about how to be healthy. We have never been less healthy. Something between the knowledge and the plate is broken." Write a talk for your class to persuade them to take their health seriously.
  2. "Schools teach algebra no pupil will use and never teach the cooking, sleeping and moving that every pupil needs." Write a talk for a school assembly arguing your point of view.
  3. "Stop blaming teenagers for their diets. Blame the industry that engineered the food and the timetable that stole the sleep." Write a talk for your year group responding to this statement.

  • Concrete contrast: "Picture two plates. One was cooked in twenty minutes from things that grew. The other was engineered in a laboratory to be impossible to stop eating. Guess which one is cheaper, faster, and advertised to you."

  • Topic bridge: the gap is not knowledge; it is environment.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (talk for your class, ~715 words) — opens with a concrete-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Picture two plates. The first was cooked in twenty minutes from things that grew in the ground — some rice, some vegetables, a piece of fish. The second was engineered over months in a laboratory, salted and sugared and softened until it became, by design, almost impossible to stop eating. Now guess which plate is cheaper. Guess which is faster. Guess which one is advertised to you, at fourteen, on the screen you are holding right now. The gap between knowing how to be healthy and actually being healthy is not made of ignorance. It is made of those two plates.

[U — Urgency]

We have built a maze where every easy turning leads to the vending machine. Sport England's 2024 Active Lives: Children and Young People survey found that thirty-six percent of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet the recommended hour of activity a day. The NHS and the Sleep Foundation both say teenagers need between eight and ten hours of sleep; most of this room got six last night. The skipped breakfast, the four-hour evening online, the short night before a long day: this is not laziness. This is an environment engineered to make the unhealthy turning the easy one.

[M1 — The plate]

A child who can cook is a child the food industry cannot fully own. We are taught algebra many of us will never use and never taught how to turn a bag of cheap ingredients into a meal that does not come from a factory. One real lesson a week — read a label, cook a single dish, taste the difference — would hand every pupil a defence the marketing cannot get past. NHS guidance on a balanced diet is freely available and almost never taught as a practical skill. The plate is where the country's health is decided, and we have stopped teaching it.

[M2 — The body]

The body is not furniture; it was built to move, and the school timetable has quietly designed the movement out. Active travel to school, a protected daily mile, PE that is not the first lesson sacrificed when the timetable is tight: each of these is cheap, each is proven, each is being cut. Sport England's data make the cost visible — a generation sitting more than any before it. Movement is medicine the country is choosing not to prescribe.

[M3 — The sleep]

The most underrated health intervention in Britain is the earliest bedtime. Sleep is the night-shift the body cannot skip, and the phone in the bedroom is the thing that steals it. The NHS recommends eight to ten hours for teenagers; the bedroom lit blue at midnight delivers six. Phones charging downstairs, a consistent bedtime, a school day that starts when the teenage body is actually awake: none of this costs money, and all of it changes a life. We treat sleep as the thing we cut to make room for everything else. It is the thing everything else depends on.

[V — Vision]

Breakfast is cooked, not skipped. The walk to school wakes a body that slept the hours it needed. Health becomes the default, not the daily battle.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say health is a personal choice, and nobody forces a teenager to eat badly. Choice exists, but it is not free. The food was engineered to be chosen, the timetable was built to exhaust, and the cheapest calorie in the shop is the least healthy one on the shelf. Reform the environment — the lessons, the timetable, the bedroom — and the choice becomes genuinely free for the first time. Right now we are blaming people for losing a game whose rules were written against them.

[C — Conclusion]

Two plates. The cooked one and the engineered one. The whole argument is that we could make the cooked plate the cheaper, faster, ordinary choice — and that the only reason we have not is that someone profits from the other one. So cook one meal this week. Walk one journey you would normally ride. Sleep one full night with the phone in another room. Then choose for yourself, freely, for once.

Q6 — Personal achievement, a turning point

Q6 — Personal achievement, a turning point

Why this is likely. OCR's Section B frequently invites first-person reflective writing, and the blog-post form — unique to OCR — suits a personal-achievement narrative addressed to regular readers. The cluster pairs with the paper's nineteenth-century memoir and self-improvement archive. This theme is narrative; lean on a vivid anecdote, not statistics.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write a post for your blog describing a personal achievement that changed how you see yourself."
  2. "Some achievements are loud. The ones that change you are usually quiet." Write a blog post arguing why the quiet achievements matter most.
  3. "Write a post for your blog about a turning point — the day you discovered you could do something you had believed you could not."

Frame. A real achievement is not the medal; it is the moment the inner story rewrites itself. The turning point is internal — the day "I cannot" became "I did" — and the blog's job is to make a reader feel that exact pivot, not merely report it.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Sensory snapshot: "My legs were shaking on the third flight of stairs, and the summit was still an hour above me. I remember thinking, with total certainty, that I was about to turn around. I remember, just as clearly, not turning around."
  • Bold statement: "The day I changed was not the day I won anything. It was the day I refused to quit something small."
  • Rhetorical question: "When was the last time you surprised yourself?"
  • Topic bridge: name the inner shift, not just the outer event.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the moment of doubt as a locked door the reader recognises.
  • Metaphor: "There is a door inside every difficult thing, and on it is written you cannot."
  • Extension: the shaking legs; the watching crowd; the voice that says turn back.
  • Evidence: personal narrative — no statistic. Use the precise physical memory instead.
  • Devices: triadic (the legs / the crowd / the voice); short pivot ("That door was the achievement.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Before. Frame: who you were on the wrong side of the door. Metaphor: "I had spent years believing the label someone handed me." Develop: the fixed self-belief, rendered concretely. Evidence: the anecdote begins here (its home — no second one).
  • M2 — The pivot. Frame: the single moment the story turned. Metaphor: "The door did not open; I leaned on it and it gave." Develop: the precise instant, slowed right down, sensory. Evidence: the turning point in full detail.
  • M3 — After. Frame: what the achievement rewrote. Metaphor: "I did not get a new skill; I got a new sentence to tell myself." Develop: how one finished thing changed the next attempt. Evidence: a later, harder thing you tried because of it.
  • Transitions: That was before. This was the moment. This is after.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "I attempt harder things now."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The label that once defined me has quietly stopped applying."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "The door stays open."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Scale objection: "It was a small thing; plenty of people do far harder."
  • Luck objection: "Maybe you would have managed it anyway; nothing really changed."
  • One-off objection: "One good day does not rewrite a person."
  • Refutation pivot: "The size of the achievement was never the point. A small finished thing teaches the same lesson a large one does — that the label was wrong. Once you have felt that once, you cannot fully un-feel it."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the shaking legs; the third flight of stairs.
  • New move: the legs steadied; the summit arrived; the certainty that you would turn back was, for the first time, wrong.
  • Final sentence: "Find the smallest thing you believe you cannot finish. Then finish it. The door is lighter than it looks."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (blog post describing a personal achievement, ~715 words) — opens with a sensory-snapshot hook

[I — Introduction]

My legs were shaking on the third flight of stone steps, and the summit was still an hour above me. I remember thinking, with total certainty, that I was about to turn around and go back down to the car park where my dad was waiting. I remember, just as clearly, not turning around. This post is about the smallest achievement I have ever had, and the largest change it ever made. If you are reading this and you have a thing you have decided you cannot finish, stay with me.

[U — Urgency]

There is a door inside every difficult thing, and written on it, in handwriting that looks exactly like your own, are the words you cannot. On Snowdon, that April, the door was made of shaking legs, a watching family, and a small flat voice in my head telling me, reasonably and patiently, to turn back. The voice was not cruel. It was worse than cruel. It was convincing. That door, and not the summit, turned out to be the achievement.

[M1 — Before]

I had spent years believing the label someone once handed me: that I was "not really a sporty one", that I gave up when things got hard, that this was simply a fact about me, like my height. I had evidence. I had quit the football team, the swimming club, the school cross-country in Year 8 with a fake stitch. By thirteen I had stopped trying things I might fail at, because failing matched the label and trying risked disproving it, which was somehow more frightening.

[M2 — The pivot]

The door did not swing open. I leaned on it and it gave. On the third flight, legs trembling, I did not make a brave decision. I made a tiny one: I would do ten more steps, then decide. I counted them. At ten I was still standing, so I did ten more. The mountain did not get easier. I simply stopped negotiating with the whole climb at once and started negotiating with ten steps. An hour of ten-step bargains later, I was standing in cloud at the top, and I was crying, and I did not fully understand why.

[M3 — After]

I did not come down with a new skill. I came down with a new sentence to tell myself, and the sentence was: the label was wrong. Three weeks later I went back to a maths topic I had written off as beyond me, and I sat with it for ten minutes, then ten more, and the bargaining worked there too. The discovery was not about mountains, or maths, or even effort. It was that the wall I kept meeting at the start of every hard thing was not made of the hard thing at all. It was made of a story I had been told once and had carried, unexamined, for years. I am not a different person. I am the same person who finally caught the old one telling a lie.

[V — Vision]

I attempt harder things now. The label that once defined me has quietly stopped applying, not because I defeated it but because I stopped believing it. The door stays open.

[C — Counter-argument]

You might say it was a small thing — a hill plenty of people climb before breakfast — and that plenty of people manage far harder. You would be right. The size of the achievement was never the point. A small finished thing teaches exactly the same lesson a large one does: that the story you were telling about yourself was a story, not a fact. Once you have felt that, once, you cannot fully un-feel it.

[C — Conclusion]

My legs were shaking on the third flight of stairs, and I was certain I would turn around. The certainty was wrong, for the first time in my life, and finding that out was worth more than the view. So here is the only advice this blog will ever give you: find the smallest thing you have decided you cannot finish. Make a ten-step bargain with it. Then finish it. The door is lighter than it looks, and your handwriting is not the truth.

Q7 — Success, advice to young people

Q7 — Success, advice to young people

Why this is likely. OCR favours the speech form for inspirational, advice-to-peers prompts, and the cluster pairs with the paper's nineteenth-century self-improvement and careers-memoir archive (Smiles, Seacole). A statement defining success against status fits a leavers' or assembly speech exactly.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Success is not what you accumulate. It is what you become, and who you carry with you on the way." Write a speech for your year group on what success really means.
  2. "Stop chasing prestige. The country needs people who do the work that holds it together — and the work that holds it together is rarely the work that pays the most." Write a speech for a careers evening arguing your point of view.
  3. "Write a speech for younger students giving them the one piece of advice you wish someone had given you."

Frame. The country has confused status with significance. Real success is the work that holds society together — teaching, nursing, building, repairing — which we pretend to admire and quietly underpay. The advice worth giving is: choose purpose, and refuse the pretence that purpose and reward must be opposites.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Bold statement: "Most of what you have been told about success is a sales pitch, and the people selling it are not the people who succeeded."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "A newly qualified secondary teacher in 2025 earns less, in real terms, than they did in 2010."
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture yourself at thirty, successful by every measure the adverts use, and quietly certain you took the wrong turning at sixteen."
  • Topic bridge: the pay packet has stopped tracking the work that matters.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: a country quietly emptying out of its essential workforce.
  • Metaphor: "We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree."
  • Extension: the GP surgery that cannot recruit; the school short five teachers in September; the bus cancelled because there is no driver.
  • Evidence: DfE and NHS England 2024 vacancy data; IFS analysis of public-sector real-terms pay erosion since 2010.
  • Devices: triadic (surgery / school / bus); short pivot ("This is not a labour market.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Define it yourself. Frame: success is a definition someone is selling you; write your own. Metaphor: "A borrowed definition fits like a borrowed coat." Develop: name what you would be proud of at forty. Evidence: Sutton Trust 2023 research on careers aspirations and social mobility.
  • M2 — Choose the useful road. Frame: the work the country needs is the work worth doing. Metaphor: "Nursing is national infrastructure; treat it like infrastructure." Develop: teaching, care, trades, engineering as aspirations not fall-backs. Evidence: DfE / Skills England apprenticeship data 2024.
  • M3 — Carry people with you. Frame: success measured alone is loneliness with a trophy. Metaphor: "A win nobody shares is a sound nobody hears." Develop: who you lift on the way up. Evidence: use a concrete example rather than a statistic — the older student who coached you, the teammate you went back for — and let the lived detail carry the point.
  • Transitions: Define it. Then choose it. Then share it.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (4 words): "Wards fill again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Sixth-formers choose teaching because the work means something, and admit it proudly."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Purpose is paid."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Reward objection: "Status and money are real rewards; do not pretend they do not matter."
  • Freedom objection: "Teenagers should be free to chase prestige if they want to."
  • Naivety objection: "Telling young people to follow purpose is comfortable advice from someone who already has security."
  • Refutation pivot: "Money matters; it is not the same as success. The richest unhappy person and the secure fulfilled one are both real. You are choosing which story you will be telling at forty — choose it on purpose, not by default."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the sales pitch returns; the people selling it.
  • New move: you write your own definition; you choose the useful road; you carry someone with you.
  • Final sentence: "Define success before someone sells you theirs. Then go and become it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech for your year group on what success really means, ~715 words) — opens with a bold-statement hook

[I — Introduction]

Most of what you have been told about success is a sales pitch, and the people selling it to you are not, on the whole, the people who actually succeeded. They are the people selling cars, watches, courses and dreams. Real success has been quietly redefined to mean accumulation, because accumulation is the thing that can be sold back to you. I want to spend the next four minutes arguing that you have been handed the wrong definition, and that the right one is waiting for you to write it.

[U — Urgency]

We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree. Across this country, the GP surgery cannot recruit. The new school is five teachers short in September. The bus some of you took this morning may have been cancelled because the depot had no driver. Department for Education and NHS England vacancy data from 2024 confirm what every household already suspects: the work that holds this country together is the work this country has quietly stopped valuing. This is not a labour market. This is a slow emptying, and your generation is being recruited to refill it.

[M1 — Define it yourself]

A borrowed definition fits like a borrowed coat — close enough that you stop noticing, wrong enough that you are never quite warm. So write your own. Ask not what job sounds impressive at a party, but what you would be genuinely proud of at forty. Sutton Trust research has established for years that teenagers, especially those from lower-income homes, are handed a narrow menu of aspirations and almost never presented the vocational roles as ambitions rather than back-ups. Widen your own menu before someone narrows it for you.

[M2 — Choose the useful road]

Nursing is national infrastructure; we should treat it like infrastructure. So is teaching, so is care work, so is the engineer who keeps the water clean and the trains running. Department for Education and Skills England data from 2024 record that degree-equivalent apprenticeships in engineering, healthcare and construction now match or exceed many graduate-route earnings within five years. The useful road is not the poor road. It is the road the adverts forgot to advertise, because nobody profits from sending you down it.

[M3 — Carry people with you]

A win nobody shares is a sound nobody hears. The version of success worth chasing is not the one measured alone at the top of a ladder, but the one measured by who you reached down for on the way up. The classmate you helped through a topic, the younger student you mentored, the colleague you made room for: these are not distractions from success. They are the part of it that lasts after the trophy tarnishes. Think of the people you most admire in your own life. I would wager that almost none of them came to mind because of what they own, and almost all of them came to mind because of what they did for someone smaller than themselves. That is the test. That is the only scoreboard that still reads true at the end.

[V — Vision]

Wards fill again. Sixth-formers choose teaching because the work means something, and they admit it proudly. Purpose is paid.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you are thinking that money and status are real rewards, and that I should not pretend they do not matter. You are right. They matter. They are simply not the same thing as success. The richest unhappy person and the secure, fulfilled one both exist; I have met both. What I am asking is that you choose which of those two stories you will be telling about your life at forty — and that you choose it on purpose, rather than letting an advert choose it for you by default.

[C — Conclusion]

So: most of what you have been told about success is a sales pitch. The people selling it are not the people who succeeded. You can spend the next decade chasing a definition someone wrote to profit from you, or you can write your own, choose the road the country actually needs, and carry someone with you while you walk it. Define success before someone sells you theirs. Then go and become it.

[I — Introduction]

Most of what you have been told about success is a sales pitch, and the people selling it to you are not, on the whole, the people who actually succeeded. They are the people selling cars, watches, courses and dreams. Real success has been quietly redefined to mean accumulation, because accumulation is the thing that can be sold back to you. I want to spend the next four minutes arguing that you have been handed the wrong definition, and that the right one is waiting for you to write it.

[U — Urgency]

We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree. Across this country, the GP surgery cannot recruit. The new school is five teachers short in September. The bus some of you took this morning may have been cancelled because the depot had no driver. Department for Education and NHS England vacancy data from 2024 confirm what every household already suspects: the work that holds this country together is the work this country has quietly stopped valuing. This is not a labour market. This is a slow emptying, and your generation is being recruited to refill it.

[M1 — Define it yourself]

A borrowed definition fits like a borrowed coat — close enough that you stop noticing, wrong enough that you are never quite warm. So write your own. Ask not what job sounds impressive at a party, but what you would be genuinely proud of at forty. Sutton Trust research has established for years that teenagers, especially those from lower-income homes, are handed a narrow menu of aspirations and almost never presented the vocational roles as ambitions rather than back-ups. They are the part of it that lasts after the trophy tarnishes. Think of the people you most admire in your own life. I would wager that almost none of them came to mind because of what they own, and almost all of them came to mind because of what they did for someone smaller than themselves. That is the test. That is the only scoreboard that still reads true at the end.

Wards fill again. Sixth-formers choose teaching because the work means something, and they admit it proudly. Purpose is paid.

Some of you are thinking that money and status are real rewards, and that I should not pretend they do not matter. You are right. They matter. They are simply not the same thing as success. You can spend the next decade chasing a definition someone wrote to profit from you, or you can write your own, choose the road the country actually needs, and carry someone with you while you walk it. Q8 — Travel complaint, consumer rights

Why this is likely. OCR's letter options include letters to a company or an employer, and the consumer-rights cluster pairs with the paper's nineteenth-century writing on commerce, travel and trade. A complaint letter is a classic OCR Section B shape: it demands controlled, formal register (AO5) and tight accuracy (AO6), and rewards a persuasive structure beneath the politeness.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write a letter to the manager of a travel company complaining about a holiday or journey that went badly wrong, and arguing for the response you expect."
  2. "Companies rely on customers being too tired to complain. The customer who writes the letter is the customer who changes the company." Write a letter to a company arguing your case after a journey went wrong.
  3. "Write a letter to a newspaper editor arguing that travel companies treat consumers as if they have no rights at all."

Frame. A complaint is not a tantrum; it is a contract being enforced. The customer who writes calmly, names the specific failure, and states the specific remedy is not being difficult — they are holding a company to the promise it sold. Politeness and pressure are not opposites.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Shocking statistic: "The UN World Tourism Organization recorded over 1.4 billion international arrivals in 2023. I was, for eleven hours on the fourteenth of August, one of them — stranded, uninformed, and treated as if my ticket had bought me nothing."
  • Bold statement: "I am not writing to complain. I am writing to enforce a contract your company appears to have forgotten it signed."
  • Contrast: "Your brochure promised a seamless journey. Your airline delivered eleven hours on a terminal floor."
  • Topic bridge: name the specific failure and the specific remedy, calmly.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the stranded customer as a contract no one will honour.
  • Metaphor: "A ticket is a promise printed on paper. Your company printed it, then walked away from it."
  • Extension: the cancelled departure; the absent staff; the helpline that looped; the night on the floor.
  • Evidence: UNWTO 2024 — 1.4bn arrivals 2023; under UK261, passengers whose flight departs a UK airport and is delayed three hours or more are entitled to between £220 and £520 depending on distance; Citizens Advice provides free consumer-rights guidance.
  • Devices: triadic (cancellation / silence / floor); short pivot ("This is not service.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The failure, precisely. Frame: name what went wrong with dates, times and facts. Metaphor: "A complaint without specifics is weather; a complaint with specifics is evidence." Develop: the exact sequence of events. Evidence: booking reference, times, names where possible.
  • M2 — The right, precisely. Frame: the consumer protections that apply. Metaphor: "Your contract is not a favour; it is a legal floor." Develop: the duty of care, the right to rerouting or refund, the right to information. Evidence: for flights, UK261 (the retained UK version of EU rules) guarantees care, rerouting or refund and fixed compensation for long delays; for rail, the Delay Repay scheme refunds 25% of the fare for delays of 15–29 minutes, rising to a full refund for delays of an hour or more.

  • Sentence 1 (6 words): "A company that honours its contracts."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "A customer who travels again, because the failure was put right."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "Trust, repaired and earned."

  • Force-majeure objection: "The disruption was outside the company's control."

[I — Introduction]

Dear Sir or Madam,

The UN World Tourism Organization recorded more than 1.4 billion international arrivals in 2023. For eleven hours on the fourteenth of August this year, I was one of them — stranded in Terminal Two, given no information, and treated as if the ticket your company sold me had purchased nothing at all. I am writing not to vent, but to enforce a contract your airline appears to have forgotten it signed. My booking reference is QX-48810.

[U — Urgency]

A ticket is a promise printed on paper. Your company printed mine, took my payment of four hundred and twenty pounds, and then, when the moment came to honour it, walked away. The cancellation was announced on a screen, not by a person. The desk that should have rebooked me was unstaffed for the entire evening. The helpline I rang eleven times looped me back to a menu that did not list "your flight has been cancelled and nobody will tell me why" as an option. By midnight I was sitting on the terminal floor with a child who should have been in a bed. This is not service. This is abandonment with a logo on it.

[M1 — The failure, precisely]

A complaint without specifics is weather; a complaint with specifics is evidence, so here are the specifics. Flight QX448 to Faro, scheduled for 19:05, was cancelled at 18:40 with no explanation. No alternative flight was offered at the gate. No accommodation was arranged, despite the next available departure being the following afternoon. No member of staff was present at the service desk between 18:40 and the time I left the terminal at 06:15. I have kept the boarding pass, the cancellation screenshot, and the times of all eleven helpline calls.

[M2 — The right, precisely]

Your contract is not a favour you grant; it is a legal floor you are required to meet. Under the consumer protections that apply to flights of this kind, a cancelled passenger is owed a clear duty of care: information, refreshments, accommodation where an overnight delay results, and the choice between rerouting and a full refund. Citizens Advice sets out these rights plainly for any consumer who looks. Your airline met none of them. The failure was not the cancellation. The failure was the eleven hours in which you behaved as though the cancellation released you from every obligation you had taken my money to assume.

[M3 — The remedy, precisely]

A fair remedy is not revenge; it is repair, and I am asking only for repair. I require, first, a full refund of the four-hundred-and-twenty-pound fare. I require, second, reasonable compensation for the overnight expenses your failure forced upon me, itemised receipts enclosed. I require, third, a written explanation of why the service desk was unstaffed, and what your company intends to change so that the next stranded family is not left on the floor I was left on.

[V — Vision]

I would like to write to you again one day to book another flight. I would like to be a customer who travels with your airline because the failure was put right, not one who warns every friend away from it. Trust can be repaired. That outcome is still available to you, for now.

[C — Counter-argument]

You may reply that the disruption was outside your control. Some of it surely was; the response to it was entirely within it. I am not holding your airline responsible for whatever grounded the aircraft. I am holding it responsible for the eleven hours of silence, the empty desk, and the looping helpline that followed. The first was misfortune. The second was a choice, and choices are exactly what a complaint is entitled to address.

[C — Conclusion]

A ticket is a promise printed on paper. You printed mine; please now honour it. I expect a written reply within fourteen days and a resolution that matches the promise your brochure made when it took my money. Enforce your own contract, before I am obliged to enforce it for you.

Yours faithfully,

[the writer]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter to a company arguing that the complaining customer changes the company) by sharpening M2 around the leverage of the written complaint, and Stem 3 (letter to a newspaper editor on consumer rights more broadly) by widening the specific complaint into a pattern. The shocking-statistic hook fits the article-style stems; for a pure complaint letter, the bold-statement open is cleaner.

Q9 — Animals, nature, our place in it

Why this is likely.   2. "Keeping a dog in a city flat is cruelty dressed up as companionship." Write a post for an online forum arguing for or against this view.

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Rhetorical question: "Someone above said zoos are arks. A genuine question for the thread: what is a zoo for, if the animal inside it has lost the instincts of its own species?"

  • M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle. Frame: the sanctuary feeds the animal first; the zoo sells the ticket first. Metaphor: "One is a hospital. The other is a theatre." Develop: named UK sanctuaries (the Born Free Foundation's rescue work; accredited sanctuaries) set against the ticket-led safari-park model. Evidence: argue from the difference in purpose — welfare-first versus turnstile-first — rather than a revenue figure.
