Model answer — written to Stem 1 (post for an online discussion forum, ~715 words) — opens with a rhetorical-question hook

[I — Introduction]

Someone a few posts up called zoos "arks saving species", and a few of you have agreed. I want to push back, genuinely, and here is the question I would put to the whole thread: what exactly is a zoo for, if the animal inside it has slowly lost the instincts of its own species? Because that is what the evidence keeps describing — not an ark, but an institution we built in the nineteenth century and have simply never had the nerve to retire.

[U — Urgency]

Every enclosure is a Victorian habit we forgot how to throw away. The keepers know it. The children sense it. The species pace it. The Born Free Foundation has documented stereotypic behaviours — the pacing, the head-bobbing, the obsessive grooming — in captive animals across British zoos for over thirty years, and the WWF Living Planet Report of 2024 records a sixty-nine percent average decline in monitored wildlife populations since 1970. Outside the cage, the species is collapsing. Inside the cage, the same species is acting out the collapse. This is not education. This is exhibition.

[M1 — The pacing]

The pacing is the lecture. Stereotypic behaviour is the body of the captive animal telling the truth our mouths will not — that the enclosure designed for our viewing is the enclosure designed against the animal's sanity. BIAZA's welfare standards have improved with each decade, and the better zoos have shrunk their elephant herds and dismantled their cetacean tanks. But improvement is not absolution. An enclosure with enrichment is still an enclosure, and a tiger taught to chase frozen meat is still a tiger redesigned for the gift shop.

[M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle]

One is a hospital. The other is a theatre. The Big Cat Sanctuary in Kent and the rescue-led sanctuaries that partner with Born Free exist to feed the animal first and sell the ticket last — and they do not pretend the visit is the point. Many of them limit visitor numbers, keep the rescued animals out of sight when those animals need quiet, and would happily close to the public tomorrow if the funding allowed it. The safari park, by contrast, is built around the family ticket: the lion sleeps where the postcard can be taken, and the gift shop opens at nine. The whole site is designed so the animal is always visible, because an invisible animal sells nothing. The two business models are not equivalent. They are opposites with the same word painted on the door, and the original poster has confused them.

[M3 — Conservation in the wild]

A reserve is a library. A zoo is a postcard. Real conservation is field-based, slow and rarely glamorous: the Knepp rewilding estate in West Sussex, the wolf reintroduction at Yellowstone, the in-situ breeding programmes that nobody buys a ticket to attend. Each returns the animal to the system it evolved inside, and the cage has never once done that. The wolves brought back to Yellowstone did not merely survive; they reshaped the rivers, because predators restored the balance that kept the banks from eroding — a result no enclosure on earth could ever produce, because an enclosure removes the animal from the very system its life is meant to mend. If you genuinely want to save a species, fund the field, not the turnstile.

[V — Vision]

The enclosure stands empty. The funding has moved to the field where the animal still belongs. Conscience caught up.

[C — Counter-argument]

The strongest reply in this thread is that children need to see real animals, or conservation loses its public. There is something in that. There is also this: children who see a captive tiger learn that captivity is acceptable. The lesson teaches the cage, not the species. The documentary, the supported field trip, the high-definition livestream from a real reserve — each does what the zoo claims to do, without asking the body of an animal to perform for it.

[C — Conclusion]

So picture, one last time, the polar bear pacing three steps left, three steps right, three steps left, the glass the only thing it cannot break. Then decide which of the two — the brochure that calls it an ark, or the animal worn down in front of you — is telling the truth. It is not an ark. It is a habit we are long overdue to break. Cages are an apology we still owe the animals we built them for. Let us finally pay it.

The same shell inverts for Stem 2 (forum post on city-flat pet ownership) by re-anchoring the M-beats in domestic animal welfare, and supports Stem 3 (forum post on rewilding over caging) by leading with M3. The rhetorical-question hook suits the forum form, which replies to an imagined thread; keep the register conversational but argued, not chatty.

Q10 — A moment that changed my life

Q10 — A moment that changed my life

Why this is likely. OCR's Section B reliably offers a reflective, first-person prompt, and the "moment that changed me" shape pairs with the paper's nineteenth-century memoir archive. The article form addressed to a general reader lets a single decisive moment carry a whole piece. This theme is narrative — lean on a vivid anecdote, not statistics.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write an article for a magazine about a single moment that changed the direction of your life."
  2. "Some lives turn on a decision. Others turn on a chance no one chose." Write an article exploring a moment that changed yours.
  3. "Write an article about the day you saw something that made you think differently about the world."

Frame. A life rarely turns on a grand event; it turns on a small moment seen, for once, clearly. The article's job is to render that moment so precisely the reader feels the hinge swing — and to draw, from the small scene, the larger change without flattening it into a moral.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture this: an ordinary Tuesday, an ordinary bus, an ordinary window — and, on the pavement outside, a thirty-second scene that quietly rewrote the next ten years of my life."
  • Sensory snapshot: "The kettle was still boiling when the phone rang. I never finished the tea. I have thought about that unfinished cup almost every week since."
  • Bold statement: "My life did not change on a famous day. It changed on a Tuesday nobody else would remember."
  • Topic bridge: name the hinge, not just the event.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the ordinary moment as a hinge disguised as nothing.
  • Metaphor: "The biggest doors swing on the smallest hinges, and you almost never hear them turn."
  • Extension: the setting; the small detail; the thing you nearly missed.
  • Evidence: personal narrative — no statistic. Use precise sensory memory.
  • Devices: triadic (the setting / the detail / the turn); short pivot ("That was the hinge.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Before. Frame: the self that walked into the moment. Metaphor: "I was certain about things I had never actually examined." Develop: the assumption you held. Evidence: the anecdote opens here (its home).
  • M2 — The moment. Frame: the scene itself, slowed right down. Metaphor: "Thirty seconds stretched into the rest of my life." Develop: render it sensorily, in full. Evidence: the turning point, in detail.
  • M3 — After. Frame: what the moment rewrote. Metaphor: "I did not learn a fact; I lost an assumption." Develop: the changed behaviour or belief. Evidence: a later choice traceable to the moment.
  • Transitions: That was before. This was the moment. This is after.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "I notice differently now."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The assumption I carried for years has quietly stopped fitting me."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "The hinge still holds."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Triviality objection: "It was too small a thing to have changed anything."
  • Memory objection: "You have probably exaggerated it in hindsight."
  • Inevitability objection: "You would have changed anyway; the moment was a coincidence."
  • Refutation pivot: "Perhaps I would have arrived at the same place by another road. But this is the road I took, and the moment is the corner where it turned. Hindsight did not invent the corner; it only let me finally see it."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the ordinary Tuesday returns.
  • New move: the moment recognised for what it was; the change fully owned.
  • Final sentence: "Watch your ordinary Tuesdays. One of them is a hinge, and you will not hear it turn until long after the door has opened."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~715 words) — opens with an imagine-scenario hook

[I — Introduction]

Picture this. An ordinary Tuesday, an ordinary bus, an ordinary rain-streaked window — and, on the pavement outside, a thirty-second scene that quietly rewrote the next ten years of my life. I was fifteen. I was not looking for a turning point; nobody ever is. That is rather the trouble with turning points. They do not announce themselves. They happen on Tuesdays, through bus windows, while you are thinking about something else entirely.

[U — Urgency]

The biggest doors swing on the smallest hinges, and you almost never hear them turn. The setting was a bus stop outside the library. The detail was an old man, soaked through, trying and failing to fold a broken umbrella with hands that would not cooperate. The thing I nearly missed was a girl my own age, a stranger to him, who stopped, took the umbrella, fixed it in four seconds, handed it back, and walked on without waiting to be thanked. That was the hinge. I did not know it yet. I sat on the bus and felt something rearrange itself.

[M1 — Before]

I was, at fifteen, certain about things I had never actually examined. Chief among them was a quiet belief that kindness was for people with time to spare — that it was a luxury, an extra, something you got round to once your own life was sorted. I was busy being fifteen and self-absorbed, and I had built, without noticing, a small private rule that other people's small troubles were not my responsibility unless someone made them mine.

[M2 — The moment]

Thirty seconds stretched into the rest of my life. The girl had not weighed it up. She had not calculated whether she had time, whether it was her place, whether the old man would thank her properly. She had simply seen a problem she could solve and solved it, the way you would catch a falling glass without deciding to. There was no audience. There was no reward. There was a broken umbrella, a pair of cold hands, and a stranger who treated a tiny trouble as obviously worth four seconds of her afternoon. The bus pulled away. I kept watching until the stop was out of sight.

[M3 — After]

I did not learn a fact that Tuesday; I lost an assumption. Kindness, I understood suddenly, was not a luxury you afforded once your life was sorted. It was a reflex you either built or did not. Within a month I had started, clumsily at first, simply doing the small fixable thing when I saw it — the held door, the picked-up dropped book, the unglamorous four-second favour. The clumsiness mattered, oddly; the first few times I helped a stranger I did it badly, self-consciously, half-expecting to be waved away. The girl at the bus stop had taught me that the awkwardness was not a reason to stop. It was simply the cost of building a reflex I had been too proud to start. Ten years on, it is the single habit I am most quietly proud of, and it began with a stranger and a broken umbrella I happened to see through glass.

[V — Vision]

I notice differently now. The assumption I carried for years — that small kindnesses were optional extras — has quietly stopped fitting me. The hinge still holds.

[C — Counter-argument]

You might reasonably say it was too small a thing to have changed anything, or that I have exaggerated it with hindsight, or that I would have grown out of my teenage self-absorption regardless. Perhaps I would have arrived at the same place by another road. But this is the road I took, and that bus stop is the corner where it turned. Hindsight did not invent the corner. It only let me, years later, finally see what had happened there.

[C — Conclusion]

An ordinary Tuesday. An ordinary bus. A broken umbrella and a stranger who fixed it in four seconds and never looked back. It changed me more than any lesson I was formally taught, precisely because nobody intended it to. So here is the only thing I would ask of anyone reading this: watch your ordinary Tuesdays. One of them is a hinge, and you will not hear it turn until long after the door has quietly opened behind you.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article on a moment of chance rather than decision) by sharpening M2 around the unchosen nature of the moment, and Stem 3 (article on a day you saw the world differently) which maps almost exactly onto Stem 1. The imagine-scenario hook fits the reflective article; this theme is narrative, so use a vivid scene, not a statistic.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Hinge / door metaphor anchored I → U → V → Conclusion. No shows. No exclamation marks outside quotes.

Cross-question reuse table — memorise the recombinations

The ten plans above share underlying moves. The same metaphor frame, hook archetype or refutation pivot reappears across multiple Qs. Memorise the recombinations and you can rebuild any plan from any other on the day.

Move Used in Reusable in
Hall-of-mirrors / industrial-comparison framing Q1 (Appearance) Q3 (Technology), Q5 (Health)
Machine / flood-already-inside-the-room metaphor Q2 (Environment) Q5 (Health), Q9 (Animals)
Casino / engineered-loss / dopamine-spend metaphor Q3 (Technology) Q1 (Appearance), Q5 (Health)
Inheritance / foundation framing Q4 (Person you admire) Q6 (Achievement), Q7 (Success)
Two-plates / two-images concrete contrast Q5 (Health) Q1 (Appearance), Q8 (Travel)
Door / you cannot / hinge framing Q6 (Achievement) Q10 (Moment), Q4 (Admire)
Status-vs-purpose / fruit-and-tree framing Q7 (Success) Q4 (Admire), Q6 (Achievement)

Ordinary-Tuesday / small-hinge framing Q10 (Moment) Q6 (Achievement), Q4 (Admire)

If exam time is tight, rehearse Q1, Q3 and Q6 first — the three with the highest 2026 probability score from the recurrence-cycle analysis, and the three that drill the OCR-distinctive forms (article, article, blog post). For broader coverage rehearse all ten. The shells are deliberately recombinant: the worst-case scenario is that OCR prints a stem you have not rehearsed; the best-case is that the frame you rehearsed for Q9 fits the stem OCR actually printed. Both scenarios are designed for. Remember too: OCR gives you Q5 OR Q6 — read both before committing, then write the one whose form and topic you can argue best.

Creative stretches — one extreme example per Q

The ten skeletons above are the SAFE shape — IUMVCC, six beats, the locked structure that consistently scores AO5 in the top bands. They will land your answer firmly in the upper grades.

To push higher, the examiner needs to see voice. Not just techniques — voice. The examples in The Complete Writer's Craft prove the range: a conversational essay that thinks aloud and is rigorously argued beneath the loose surface; a vivid description that builds one long controlled image; an argument made entirely through contrast. One Sophicly student once wrote a film review in a character's own invented voice — the inventive form was a perfect AO5 reward, because the form FIT the topic. None of these is a template to copy. They are proof that the opening can be anything, provided it earns its place.

The rule for breaking the rules. A creative stretch only works if the voice or form choice fits the topic. A playful persona can review a blockbuster; it cannot mourn a funeral or complain to an airline. Always ask: does this voice ARGUE the topic, or does it just decorate it? If it argues, ship it. If it decorates, cut it. Never reach for a clever voice because it impressed you somewhere else — reach for the one this question needs. (And never write "in the voice of" a named real person.)

Below, one stretch idea per Q. Use them as inspiration; do NOT mimic them verbatim.

Q1 — Appearance: write as the filter

Stretch idea. Persona-shift. Write the article from the point of view of the beauty filter itself, narrating its day shaping the faces of every teenager who opens the app. The filter is enthusiastic, professional, slightly proud — until the closing paragraph, where it admits it has never met any of the faces it sells, and never will.

Why it works. The instrument of the harm narrates itself. The filter's cheerful professionalism is the indictment — examiner surprise plus AO5 inventive structure in one move.

Risk to manage. The filter's voice must not slide into the student's. The filter does not moralise — it SELLS. The argument lands by what the filter is too oblivious to see.

Q2 — Environment: write as a 2050 weather forecaster

Stretch idea. Frame the speech as a 2050 weather forecast. The forecaster reads out "Tuesday — forty-one degrees in Manchester, forty-three in London." The audience realises, slowly, that the forecast describes a different country. Bathos does the moral work: alongside the catastrophic numbers, the forecaster reminds viewers to "peg the washing somewhere shady".

Why it works. Speech is the locked form. The forecast persona makes the abstract concrete, and the bathos does the moral work in one line: the country has normalised the unsurvivable.

Risk to manage. The bathos must SERVE the argument. End by breaking character: "Or — we could choose a different forecast. We still have time to write the script of a different decade."

Q3 — Technology: write the first half AI-bland, then break character

Stretch idea. Write the first three paragraphs in deliberate machine-bland — "In today's society, technology has become an increasingly important topic, with many things to consider." Then the student voice arrives: "That paragraph took a machine four seconds. It took your concentration eight. We are three paragraphs in and nothing has been argued. This is exactly what the technology does to writing."

Why it works. The meta move makes the argument by demonstrating it. The answer that opens by mocking the very thing it warns about is the one the examiner remembers.

Risk to manage. Only attempt this if you can sustain flat, vague prose for three paragraphs WITHOUT accidentally writing well. Practise the bland.

Q4 — Person you admire: write the letter as a single returned object

Stretch idea. Structure the letter around one object the admired person once gave or left you — a book with a margin note, a worn-out football, a recipe card. Each IUMVCC beat returns to the object, until by the conclusion the object has become the lesson it carried.

Why it works. The object anchors abstract admiration in something the reader can hold. The letter persuades through a thing, not an assertion.

Risk to manage. Keep the object literal until the end; let the meaning accumulate. Do not explain the symbolism — let the final beat reveal it.

Q5 — Health: write the talk as two plates speaking

Stretch idea. Personify the two plates from the contrast hook. The cooked plate and the engineered plate argue across the talk about which one the audience will choose, the engineered plate confident and well-funded, the cooked plate honest and ignored — until the audience realises which voice has been winning their evenings.

Why it works. The personified contrast makes "diet" stop being an abstract noun and start being a conversation the audience can overhear themselves losing.

Risk to manage. Keep both plates' voices distinct and short. The moment the conceit runs long it becomes a sketch. Break out into the direct call to action at the end.

Q6 — Achievement: write as a countdown of ten steps

Stretch idea. Structure the blog post as ten numbered steps — the literal ten-step bargain from the climb — each step a short section that doubles as a beat of the larger reflection. By step ten, the reader realises the structure has been the argument: any large thing is just ten small things, ten times.

Why it works. The form enacts the message. The structure is not decoration; it is the point being made.

Risk to manage. The steps must still THINK, not just count. Each must carry a real beat of reflection, or the gimmick collapses.

Q7 — Success: write the speech as two job adverts read aloud

Stretch idea. Open with two job adverts read side by side. The first: "Wanted: someone to hold a ward together through a night shift. Pay: less, in real terms, than in 2010. Reward: a life that mattered." The second: "Wanted: someone to move money between accounts that already have too much. Pay: more than you can spend. Reward: to be decided." Then break into the IUMVCC argument.

Why it works. The paired-advert structure makes the contrast the whole country lives inside instantly visible — the same labour market, two value systems, read in one breath.

Risk to manage. The adverts must stay tight and parallel. If the joke runs long it becomes a sketch. Two short adverts, then out.

Q8 — Travel complaint: write as the unredeemed ticket

Stretch idea. Personify the ticket itself — the printed promise from the Introduction. The ticket narrates its short life: bought with hope, scanned at the gate, then abandoned on a terminal floor when the company walked away from the promise it represented. The ticket is calm, factual, and quietly damning.

Why it works. Apostrophe (a personified object as speaker) is a top-band AO5 technique. The ticket-as-contract makes the breach physical, not abstract — and the formal letter register can frame the conceit without losing AO6 control.

Risk to manage. A complaint letter needs precision and a clear remedy. Do not let the conceit swallow the specifics — the dates, the refund demand, the fourteen-day deadline must survive intact.

Q9 — Animals: write as the polar bear's enclosure

Stretch idea. Persona-shift to the enclosure itself — the glass, the painted rock, the moat. The enclosure narrates the years of bears it has held, matter-of-fact and slightly ashamed, knowing it was built to display rather than to protect. Sustain the forum register: the enclosure is "replying" to the original poster who called it an ark.

Why it works. Personifying the cage makes captivity stop being an abstract debate and start being a confession from the structure responsible. Examiner surprise plus AO5 inventive structure.

Risk to manage. Keep the enclosure's voice restrained and observational. The moment it lectures, the conceit breaks. Let the detail — the worn track in the floor, the smudged glass — do the persuading.

Q10 — A moment that changed me: write the moment in present tense, frozen

Stretch idea. Write the entire article in the present tense, holding the thirty-second moment frozen, and circle back to it again and again from different distances — the bus window, fifteen-year-old you, twenty-five-year-old you — until the single frozen image has been turned over completely and its full meaning extracted.

Why it works. The frozen present tense makes the hinge-moment feel as alive on the page as it was in memory. Circling it enacts how a turning point keeps revealing itself over years.

Risk to manage. Present tense across a whole piece is hard to control (AO6). Keep tenses consistent and deliberate; do not slip into the past by accident halfway through.

How to use these stretches in the exam

  • Do not attempt a stretch unless you have rehearsed it once. Cold improvisation under timed conditions is risky.
  • The IUMVCC beats stay. Even the most inventive persona piece still has an introduction, an argument, a counter-argument and a conclusion. The persona is the surface; the structure underneath is unchanged.
  • One stretch only. Pick one frame; sustain it.
  • If the topic does not fit, ship the safe skeleton. A clean upper-band answer beats a confused middle-band one every single time.
  • Match the stretch to the OCR form. A blog post and a forum post invite a conversational persona; a complaint letter does not. Choose the stretch the printed form can carry.

Final pre-submission checklist

Before you put the pen down, run this checklist in your head. Every item costs marks if it slips.

  • ✓ You chose Q5 OR Q6 — and read both before committing.
  • ✓ Six IUMVCC beats present and named in order.
  • ✓ Word count 650+ (target 650–850). Under 650 = AO5 penalty; over 850 = control loses.
  • ✓ Hook from one of the eight archetypes — not a generic opening.
  • ✓ Form and audience matched precisely (article ≠ talk ≠ speech ≠ letter ≠ blog post ≠ forum post).
  • ✓ One extended metaphor anchored across at least three beats (I → U → Conclusion at minimum).
  • ✓ Triadic structure used with parallel grammar in U and Conclusion at minimum.
  • ✓ Conclusion echoes the Introduction image (circular structure rewards AO5).
  • ✓ Direct address (you, we, us) used for solidarity, not scattered mechanically.
  • ✓ Every statistic real and citeable; any gap marked [VERIFY-NEEDED], never stated as fact.
  • ✗ No shows / showing / shown.
  • ✗ No abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to).
  • ✗ No discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly, Secondly, Finally).
  • ✗ No really, very, just, basically.
  • ✗ No Americanisms (move as noun, gotten, trash).
  • ✗ No author impersonation ("in the voice of [name]").
  • ✗ Fewer than ten to be verbs (is, are, was, were).
  • ✗ No more than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.

Tick every line. The exam is forty marks. The pen is the only thing between you and them.
